food_subgroup: Noodle soup dishes with meat and egg

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_00002572 covers beef noodle soup as a category. No specific Lanzhou-style entry with egg and drink exists; closest_parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FKP covers noodle soup (beef-based) as a category. The specific Lanzhou variant with egg and drink is not individually coded; closest_parent applied.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept exists for Lanzhou beef noodle soup. SNOMED CT does not enumerate regional Chinese noodle dishes at this specificity.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of Lanzhou beef noodles with braised beef slices, one egg, broth, and an accompanying drink, estimated at approximately 650 g total.

nutrient_source.title: Beef noodle soup, Chinese-style — composite estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for hand-pulled wheat noodles (cooked), braised beef, beef broth, hard-boiled egg, radish, and chilli oil, weighted by typical Lanzhou bowl composition
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for Lanzhou beef noodles with egg and drink as a composite dish. Values are estimated by combining USDA SR Legacy data for the primary components: cooked wheat noodles (~200 g), braised beef slices (~80 g), beef bone broth (~300 g), one hard-boiled egg (~50 g), radish (~20 g). The accompanying drink is unspecified and its nutrient contribution is excluded from per-100 g values (treated as a separate beverage component). Per-100 g values are derived from the weighted average of the solid-plus-broth bowl (approximately 650 g edible weight excluding drink). Confidence is medium due to estimation.

gi_source.title: Noodles, wheat-based, boiled — GI value from University of Sydney GI Database; analogous estimate applied to Lanzhou beef noodle soup bowl
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Lanzhou beef noodles as a composite dish. The GI of 47 is derived from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for boiled wheat noodles (GI ~47, low category). The presence of beef broth, protein from beef and egg, and fat from chilli oil further attenuates the glycemic response relative to plain noodles. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × available carbohydrate per serving in grams) / 100 = (47 × 71.5) / 100 ≈ 33.6, rounded to 33.0. Available carbohydrate per serving estimated at 71.5 g (11.0 g per 100 g × 650 g serving).

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from beef bone broth and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size or request reduced-sodium broth, as a single bowl of Lanzhou beef noodles may contribute approximately 1,300–1,800 mg sodium, representing 57–78% of the recommended daily limit of 2,300 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from wheat noodles, partially offset by protein and fat
type2_diabetes.guidance: The low GI of wheat noodles (GI ~47) and the protein and fat content from beef and egg help moderate postprandial glucose rise, but the large carbohydrate portion (approximately 71 g per serving) results in a moderate-to-high glycemic load; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consider a smaller noodle portion and monitor blood glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from beef and egg yolk, and high sodium from broth
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The combination of saturated fat from braised beef and egg yolk and the high sodium content of the broth presents a moderate cardiovascular risk; individuals with cardiovascular disease should request lean beef cuts, limit broth consumption, and avoid added chilli oil to reduce saturated fat and sodium intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Lanzhou beef noodles with beef, egg, and drink is a signature set meal version of the iconic Lanzhou-style hand-pulled noodle soup (兰州牛肉面), originating from Lanzhou city in Gansu Province, northwestern China. The dish features freshly hand-pulled wheat noodles cooked to order and served in a rich, clear beef bone broth seasoned with a proprietary spice blend that typically includes Sichuan pepper, ginger, and dried chillies. The bowl is topped with thin slices of braised beef, a soft- or hard-boiled egg, thinly sliced white radish, spring onion, coriander, and a drizzle of chilli oil. This set meal version includes an accompanying drink, which varies by restaurant. The noodles have a chewy, springy texture characteristic of hand-pulled dough, and the broth is savoury, mildly spiced, and deeply umami. Lanzhou beef noodles are one of China's most widely consumed noodle dishes and have gained significant popularity in Singapore through specialist restaurants. Nutritionally, the dish provides moderate protein from beef and egg, complex carbohydrates from wheat noodles, and a notable sodium load from the broth. The low GI of wheat noodles (~47) partially moderates postprandial glucose response, but the high sodium content makes portion control important for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The egg adds cholesterol and additional protein. This dish is suitable for halal diets when prepared with halal-certified beef and is dairy-free.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for Lanzhou beef noodles with egg and drink as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data weighted by typical bowl composition. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for boiled wheat noodles. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-level data sources are high-quality and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: The food name in the input JSON ('Signature beef noodles with both beef and egg and drick') contains a typographical error ('drick' instead of 'drink'). The canonical food name has been corrected to 'Lanzhou beef noodles with beef, egg, and drink'. The accompanying drink is unspecified in the source listing; its nutrient contribution is excluded from per-100 g values as it is treated as a separate beverage component of the set meal. Trans fat is reported as 0.0; this is confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy data for the primary components (boiled wheat noodles, braised beef, beef broth, boiled egg) — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component sources used for this composite dish. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component sources used for this composite dish. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component sources used for this composite dish. The is_halal flag is set to true based on the restaurant context (Lan Ting Xu Lanzhou Beef Noodles), but halal certification status should be verified directly with the restaurant. Serving size of 650 g is an estimate for the complete bowl including broth; actual restaurant portion may vary. GI source is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this composite dish was found in the University of Sydney GI Database.

Constructed per-100 g nutrient values via USDA SR Legacy component estimation with full methodology documentation.
Added GI value (47) as analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database for boiled wheat noodles with full estimation basis documented.
Added defensible FoodOn and FoodEx2 ontology anchors with honest closest_parent match types and gap notes.
Added all three required health context conditions with specific clinical guidance.

Reformatted the 'notes' field from a numbered list to a continuous string of sentences, ensuring all -1 sentinel values (vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, selenium_mcg) are explicitly documented with their missing-data basis, as required by g3_numeric_hygiene.
